page.content: The first table presents prescribing indicators from the World Health Organization (1993), showing that the average number of medicines prescribed per encounter is 4.01 (1.406) overall, 3.27 (1.140) in hospitals, and 4.25 (1.400) in health centres/clinics, with 59.9% of prescriptions containing antibiotics, 48.2% in hospitals, and 63.7% in health centres/clinics. Furthermore, 79.2% of medicines are prescribed by generic name, with 61.4% in hospitals and 83.7% in health centres/clinics. The percentage of encounters with an injection prescribed is 22.9% overall, 19.8% in hospitals, and 24.0% in health centres/clinics, while 88.1% of medicines prescribed are from the Essential Medicines List (EML), comprising 78.3% in hospitals and 90.6% in health centres/clinics. Additionally, 4.9% of prescriptions include drug combinations known to interact, with 2.5% in hospitals and 5.7% in health centres/clinics. The average number of diagnoses per clinical encounter is 1.69 (0.739) overall, 1.44 (0.600) in hospitals, and 1.78 (0.765) in health centres/clinics. The second table outlines causes of mortality with age selection regression, indicating that for all causes, the smoothing method is applied with age + age squared for men and women aged 35+; for cancers, the same method applies with RR35–39 = 1 for men aged 35+ and RR40–44 = 1 for women aged 40+. The vascular category uses age + age squared for men aged 35+ and women aged 40+. Respiratory causes apply age + age squared for both men and women aged 40+, while external causes use age + age squared with age selections of 35+ for men and 40+ for women.
